request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"rethinkdb::devtools","name":"rethinkdb","one_liner":"Free, open-source distributed document-oriented database designed for real-time query push updates, backed by Y Combinator, that failed to convert developer love into a sustainable commercial business.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"The new pitch is a paid B2B SaaS (CLI + hosted control plane) with a clear recurring-revenue model. RethinkDB was free and open-source with no disclosed commercial monetization layer — the Trusted facts list monetization as 'other' and price_point as 'free'. Both target developers, but the revenue architectures are fundamentally different."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both operate in the developer-tooling / infrastructure space and target developers (confirmed by Trusted facts: customer_type=developer, geography=global). The new pitch addresses cloud-native DevOps workflows (preview environments), while RethinkDB addressed database infrastructure. Adjacent markets with a shared buyer persona but distinct problem domains."},"gtm":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both relied on developer-first, bottoms-up adoption: open-source community traction for RethinkDB, CLI-led self-serve for the new pitch. The distribution philosophy is similar — win individual developers first, then expand. However, RethinkDB never successfully layered a commercial GTM on top, which is precisely the risk the new pitch must navigate."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"RethinkDB was early-stage throughout most of its life (founded 2009, shutdown 2016 after ~7 years without reaching scale). The new pitch appears to be at a comparable early/pre-scale stage. Both raised seed/early funding targeting developers, though the new pitch's B2B SaaS model implies a faster path to measurable ARR than RethinkDB's open-source approach ever offered."}},"why_similar":"Both companies share the same core buyer (developers, globally) and use a developer-experience-first go-to-market approach. RethinkDB relied on community adoption and grassroots developer enthusiasm — exactly the motion a CLI-led tool like the new pitch will depend on. Both operate in cloud-native infrastructure and face the challenge of converting free/self-serve usage into paying enterprise or team accounts. The 7-year runway RethinkDB consumed without finding a monetization model is a direct cautionary parallel for any developer-infrastructure play.","where_diverged":"1. Monetization model: The new pitch ships a paid hosted control plane (B2B SaaS, subscription/usage-based) from day one; RethinkDB was permanently free with no commercial tier, which is the root cause of its collapse. 2. Product category: ephemeral preview environments (CI/CD workflow tooling) vs. distributed document database — different technical domains and buyer workflows. 3. Business model clarity: The new pitch has an explicit commercial product (the hosted control plane) separate from the CLI, giving it a concrete upsell surface RethinkDB lacked entirely.","failure_causes":["inability to monetize open-source adoption","no commercial product or paid tier ever launched","free price point with no path to revenue","developer love without enterprise willingness to pay","prolonged runway burn without sustainable unit economics","community adoption mistaken for business traction"],"lessons_for_input":["Treat the free CLI as a top-of-funnel acquisition channel, not a product — ensure the hosted control plane is the monetizable core from day one.","Define willingness-to-pay signals early: instrument the CLI to identify power users and convert them to paid control-plane seats before runway shortens.","Avoid the open-source trap: keep proprietary value (e.g., multi-cloud orchestration, SSO, audit logs) locked behind the paid tier so community traction translates to ARR.","Set explicit revenue milestones tied to fundraising checkpoints so developer enthusiasm does not mask the absence of paying customers for years.","Build enterprise-grade features (RBAC, compliance, SLA) into the roadmap early — ephemeral environments are a team/org-level concern and enterprise buyers justify higher ACVs than individual developers."]}
